video_title: Chinese Stocks in Hong Kong Near Bear Market | The China Show 6/22/2026
gist: MSCI China is entering a bear market driven almost entirely by Chinese tech software/consumer names, while the AI hardware supply chain and onshore A-shares outperform sharply; meanwhile a fragile US-Iran de-escalation pulls oil back below $80, easing inflation fears even as a surprisingly hawkish Fed under Kevin Warsh and a weak yen reshape EM rate paths.

themes.theme: China bifurcation: AI hardware vs software/consumer

insights.claim: MSCI China is entering bear-market territory driven almost entirely by Chinese tech (software/consumer) names, while Chinese bank stocks trade at 52-week highs, masking a two-speed market.
insights.speaker: Anthony (Bloomberg markets reporter)
insights.so_what: Index-level bearishness overstates the damage; the drag is concentrated in software/consumer, so investors should distinguish the benchmark from the still-rallying old-economy banks and AI hardware.
insights.quote: IT IS ONLY THE TECATE THAT ARE DRAGGING THE MARKET SO BADLY. THE BANKS ARE AT 52 WEEK HIGHS.

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs is sticking with onshore A-shares over offshore China, citing CSI 300 earnings growth of ~11% in Q2 versus 0% earnings growth for MSCI China, powered by AI profits shifting toward hardware.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: The earnings split is quantified: onshore hardware-heavy indices are compounding earnings while offshore software-heavy China is flat, supporting a long A-shares / underweight MSCI China posture.
insights.quote: WHEN IT COMES TO 11% IN SECOND QUARTER FOR CSI300. ON THE FLIP SIDE THEY TOOK MSCI CHINA EARNINGS GROWTH. THAT'S 0% IN THE SECOND QUARTER.

insights.claim: Korean market leads suggest AI hardware will keep outperforming software in China, with consumer confidence and festival spending showing no improvement despite the AI boom.
insights.speaker: Anthony (Bloomberg markets reporter)
insights.so_what: The AI capex/buildout megatrend is not filtering into Chinese consumption, so the hardware-over-software trade persists and consumer/software names stay under pressure.
insights.quote: YOU'RE GOING TO SEE HARDWARE CONTINUE TO DO BETTER. -- DO BETTER THAN SOFTWARE. FOR CHINA, THAT IS A MEGATREND.

insights.claim: China-listed AI software winners (optical fiber, optics, AI-related stocks) need to be added to the main index before their rally becomes visible in MSCI China; until then the benchmark understates the new-economy gains.
insights.speaker: guest, senior Asia equities reporter
insights.so_what: A structural index-composition lag means benchmark investors miss new-China upside; active or thematic exposure is required to capture the rally before index inclusion.
insights.quote: A LOT OF THESE NAMES NEED TO BECOME PART OF THE MAIN INDEX, OTHERWISE THE RALLY WOULD REMAIN BELOW MSCI CHINA. IT WON'T BE VISIBLE IN THE BENCHMARK.

insights.claim: The 'old China' (consumer discretionary, property) is sinking with the weak economy while 'new China' (AI-related stocks) decouples, breaking the historical pattern where Chinese markets rallied despite a weak macro.
insights.speaker: guest, senior Asia equities reporter
insights.so_what: Investors can no longer treat China as a single beta; the macro now actually drags the old-economy half, so dispersion trades (long new-China AI, short old-economy) have structural support.
insights.quote: THE OLD CHINA PART OF IT IS SINKING WITH THE ECONOMY... THE OLD ECONOMY, THE CONSUMER, CONSUMER DISCRETION PART, PROPERTY PART. THAT REMAINS WEAK.

insights.claim: Chinese tech consumer names like Baidu, Meituan and Alibaba fell 2-3% as investors questioned whether heavy AI spending can be monetized, dragging Hang Seng down ~1.5%.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: AI capex without clear monetization is being penalized in big-cap Chinese tech, a risk to watch ahead of these names' earnings.
insights.quote: BAIDU DOWN SOME 2%, MEITUAN, ALIBABA DOWN SOME 3% THIS MORNING.

themes.theme: China AI models: race, monetization and losses

insights.claim: Zhipu, which IPO'd in January, will lose between $600M and $700M in 2026 with losses widening into 2027, as consensus operating-loss estimates have widened 70% since listing because agentic AI is a loss-leading product.
insights.speaker: Robert Lea (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: A sharp disconnect between Zhipu's vertical share-price rally and worsening fundamentals flags downside risk once retail enthusiasm fades; agentic AI adoption widens losses rather than profits.
insights.quote: THIS COMPANY WILL LOSE BETWEEN 600 MILLION AND 700 MILLION U.S. DOLLARS THIS YEAR. THAT WILL WIDEN INTO 2027.

insights.claim: China now has around 900 large language models, an extreme oversupply that keeps monetization and the commercial outlook deeply in question even as model quality is high.
insights.speaker: Robert Lea (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: Fragmentation of ~900 LLMs means a brutal monetization fight; bet on ecosystem owners and hardware, not standalone model developers burning cash.
insights.quote: AT THE LAST COUNT, AROUND 900 LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS IN CHINA. 900.

insights.claim: The US AI model performance premium narrowed to about 9% in May from a typical 10-15%, with Alibaba the leading Chinese model that month, signaling a tight two-horse race for mainstream tasks.
insights.speaker: Robert Lea (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: The closing model gap supports a China-AI re-rating thesis for mainstream tasks, though US still leads at the frontier.
insights.quote: THE PERFORMANCE PREMIUM ENJOYED BY U.S. MODELS IS TYPICALLY 10% TO 15%. THAT ACTUALLY NARROWED TO ABOUT 9% IN MAY WHICH IS QUITE SOMETHING.

insights.claim: The US will retain the AI model-performance lead for roughly the next year-plus because of unlimited chip access, but China's improving semiconductors will keep narrowing the gap.
insights.speaker: Robert Lea (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: Chip access is the binding constraint; track Chinese semiconductor progress (e.g. Huawei advances) as the leading indicator for when the model gap closes.
insights.quote: THE U.S. WILL STILL MAINTAIN THE LEAD BECAUSE THEY HAVE UNLIMITED ACCESS TO THESE CHIPS.

insights.claim: Hardware will keep outperforming software in China because everything, especially AI models, runs on chips, and China is pushing toward semiconductor self-sufficiency with heavy state support.
insights.speaker: Robert Lea (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: Tangible, chip-centric hardware exposure is the higher-conviction China-AI trade versus cash-burning model developers.
insights.quote: EVERYTHING IS RUNNING ON SHIPS THESE DAYS, PARTICULARLY MODELS.

insights.claim: Zhipu's new language model ranked top on some international benchmarks for agentic coding, triggering brokerage upgrades and back-to-back gains of ~20% and ~13%, driven by retail money into smaller AI names.
insights.speaker: Robert Lea (Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: Retail-driven momentum in small-cap Chinese AI names can run far ahead of fundamentals, creating both squeeze and reversal risk in the Zhipu/MiniMax pair.
insights.quote: ON SOME INTERNATIONAL BENCHMARKS, IT WAS RANKED TOP AS FAR AS THE AGENTIC CODING ABILITIES AND THAT IS WHAT CATALYZE THE EXCITEMENT

themes.theme: China AI development model and policy

insights.claim: China's government-guided AI model focuses on integrating AI into existing business ecosystems to improve margins and efficiency rather than replacing labor, giving it higher visibility and lower social disruption than the West's hardware/labor-displacement model.
insights.speaker: Cusson (CIO, KGI International Wealth Management)
insights.so_what: A different China-AI thesis: efficiency/margin uplift for incumbent ecosystem owners with state backing, implying steadier earnings paths than Western disruptors.
insights.quote: IN CHINA, THE MODEL IS HOW TO INTEGRATE THE AI MODEL INTO THE ECOSYSTEM. HOW TO HELP THE EXISTING BUSINESS TO IMPROVE MARGIN AND IMPROVE EFFICIENCY.

insights.claim: China's big internet giants with large ecosystems incorporating AI into existing businesses will be the ultimate AI-race winners, analogous to who profited most from the internet being the best users of it, not its inventors.
insights.speaker: Cusson (CIO, KGI International Wealth Management)
insights.so_what: Favors downstream AI application names and big-cap ecosystem owners over model builders as the durable winners.
insights.quote: THOSE WHO MADE THE BEST USE OF INTERNET MADE THE MOST MONEY OFF THAT.

insights.claim: The biggest short-term risk for Hong Kong/China equities is a self-reinforcing capital-outflow cycle, as money rotates to outperforming rest-of-Asia and keeps dragging the China market down, echoing the post-COVID years.
insights.speaker: Cusson (CIO, KGI International Wealth Management)
insights.so_what: Watch fund flows as the key risk variable; a reflexive outflow loop could extend China underperformance regardless of fundamentals.
insights.quote: WHAT WE ARE AFRAID WILL BE A CYCLE WHERE CAPITAL GOES OUT OF HONG KONG AND CHINA, AND THAT CYCLE KEEPS THE MARKET GOING DOWN.

insights.claim: A future technology/model breakthrough on the scale of DeepSeek is seen as the major catalyst that would prevent investors from staying structurally short the Hong Kong/China market.
insights.speaker: Cusson (CIO, KGI International Wealth Management)
insights.so_what: An unpredictable model-breakthrough catalyst caps downside conviction for shorts; positioning should respect tail upside from Chinese AI advances.
insights.quote: EVER SINCE CHINA LAUNCHED THE DEEPSEEK MODEL, I THINK THE DEVELOPMENT OF THE TECHNOLOGY AND THE MODEL JUST KEPT GOING ON.

insights.claim: Without additional consumption stimulus and broad social-welfare reform, China's consumer market and sentiment are unlikely to turn around, with AI displacement risk feeding youth-unemployment anxiety amid a top-down diffusion directive.
insights.speaker: Johanna (Head of Emerging Markets, Citigroup)
insights.so_what: Consumption malaise is policy-dependent; fade China consumer recovery hopes absent fiscal/welfare action.
insights.quote: WITHOUT ADDITIONAL CONSUMPTION STIMULUS, BROAD-BASED SOCIAL WELFARE REFORM, IT'S HARD TO SEE A BIG TURNAROUND HERE ON THE CONSUMER MARKET

themes.theme: Oil, Strait of Hormuz and the US-Iran de-escalation

insights.claim: Brent fell below $80 (toward ~$79) and WTI to ~$75 as US-Iran technical talks continuing through the week threw cold water on Hormuz-closure fears, a bearish signal for oil.
insights.speaker: Stephen (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Continuation of talks compresses the geopolitical risk premium; oil-sensitive risk assets and EM importers benefit, oil producers fade.
insights.quote: THE FACT THAT THESE NEGOTIATIONS ARE CONTINUING IS A BEARISH SIGNAL FOR THE MARKET.

insights.claim: Only ~8 million barrels of oil moved through the Strait of Hormuz over Saturday and Sunday combined versus 20 million barrels per day before the war, so flows remain far below normal despite Iranian rhetoric.
insights.speaker: Stephen (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Physical flows, not headlines, are the real tell; with throughput still a fraction of pre-war levels, supply normalization is partial and reversible.
insights.quote: BLOOMBERG HAS COUNTED ABOUT 8 MILLION BARRELS OF OIL HAS GONE THROUGH THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ OVER THE LAST TWO DAYS... THAT COMPARES TO 20 MILLION BARRELS PER DAY BEFORE THE WAR BEGAN.

insights.claim: A sharp drop in Chinese oil imports over the prior month helped balance the market during the disruption; whether Chinese demand rebounds is the key swing factor, but there is no immediate sign China is buying the dip.
insights.speaker: Stephen (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Chinese restocking at ~$80 (versus ~$120 in March) is the upside demand risk for oil; absence of buying so far keeps near-term prices soft.
insights.quote: THE FACT CHINESE IMPORTS DROPPED SO MUCH IN THE LAST MONTH OR SO HELP BALANCE THE MARKET.

insights.claim: Iran's foreign minister said the oil/petrochemical export blockade has been lifted and some frozen assets released, with a ~$300 billion reconstruction and development plan launched for Iran and major progress toward ending the Lebanon war.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: If durable, blockade lifting plus a $300bn reconstruction plan adds supply and stability that pressures oil lower and reprices regional risk.
insights.quote: HE MENTIONED SOME FROZEN ASSETS HAVE BEEN RELEASED... AT LEAST $300 BILLION. SO THAT PLAN HAS BEEN LAUNCHED FOR IRAN RIGHT NOW.

insights.claim: The Iran deal is only a ~60-day MOU phase that could end in a deal or no deal, so the market should expect more oil volatility over that window rather than a settled peace.
insights.speaker: Cusson (CIO, KGI International Wealth Management)
insights.so_what: The de-escalation is fragile and time-boxed; hedge against renewed oil spikes over the 60-day negotiation rather than fully unwinding risk premium.
insights.quote: ANYTHING POTENTIALLY COULD HAPPEN OVER THE NEXT 60 DAYS. THERE COULD BE A DEAL OR NO DEAL.

insights.claim: Israel's exclusion from the US-Iran talks and its stated intent to maintain forces in southern Lebanon is the key powder-keg risk that could reignite the conflict despite peace efforts.
insights.speaker: Nicholas (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: The Lebanon/Israel flank is the main de-confliction failure point; an escalation there is the cleanest catalyst to re-spike oil.
insights.quote: THE ISRAELIS WERE SAYING THEY ARE GOING TO MAINTAIN THEIR POSITION IN SOUTHERN LEBANON SO I THINK THERE'S A LOT THAT THREATENS TO BLOW IN POWDER KEG UP AGAIN

insights.claim: Trump is motivated to wrap up the Iran conflict quickly because he does not want oil prices spiking through the US midterm elections this year.
insights.speaker: Jill (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: A political deadline (midterms) biases US policy toward de-escalation and lower oil, a structural dovish-for-oil factor for the year.
insights.quote: HE DOES NOT WANT OIL PRICES TO CONTINUE SPIKING THROUGH THE U.S. MIDTERM ELECTIONS THIS YEAR.

themes.theme: Central banks, FX and the hawkish Fed regime shift

insights.claim: New Fed chair Kevin Warsh surprised markets by being hawkish rather than dovish, with 9 of 18 dots pointing to a hike and emphasis on price stability, marking a regime shift.
insights.speaker: Johanna (Head of Emerging Markets, Citigroup)
insights.so_what: A hawkish Warsh Fed with half the dots flagging hikes reprices the front end, supports the dollar, and constrains EM central banks' room to ease.
insights.quote: KEVIN WARSH WAS NOT PRECOMMITTED TO A VIEW BUZZ WAS SURPRISINGLY HAWKISH. DOT LOTS, NINE OUT OF 18 TALKING ABOUT A HIKE.

insights.claim: Citi's house view is for the Fed to stay dovish as oil cools and the labor market weakens, but with Atlanta GDP tracking ~3% growth not showing in the data, the path of least resistance is to do nothing and delay moves.
insights.speaker: Johanna (Head of Emerging Markets, Citigroup)
insights.so_what: Resilient US growth data argues for Fed inertia; positioning for near-term cuts is premature, with any clarity likely to delay rather than accelerate easing.
insights.quote: THE PATH OF LEAST RESISTANCE IS TO DO NOTHING NOW. IF ANYTHING, ALL OF THIS ANY KIND OF CLARITY ON THE NEXT POLICY MOVE WILL BE TO DELAY THE MOVES.

insights.claim: EM central bank reactions to lower oil diverge by country: net energy importers that pass through energy prices may reverse hawkishness, while Korea hiked on a positive output gap unrelated to oil, requiring country-by-country differentiation.
insights.speaker: Johanna (Head of Emerging Markets, Citigroup)
insights.so_what: Trade EM rates on the specific driver (FX, pass-through, output gap), not a single oil narrative; e.g. Korea hikes stay while pass-through importers may ease.
insights.quote: WE NEED TO DISTINGUISH WHICH COUNTRY IS HIKING FOR WHAT REASON.

insights.claim: Citi switched its India central-bank call to no hike from two hikes because the oil-price decline cuts current-account pressure and capital-flow measures should stabilize the rupee.
insights.speaker: Johanna (Head of Emerging Markets, Citigroup)
insights.so_what: A concrete EM rates change: lower oil removes the hiking pressure for India, supportive for India duration and the currency.
insights.quote: THE ONE COMPANY WHERE WE CHANGE OUR CENTRAL BANK CALL RIGHT AWAY... NOW WE SAID NO HIKE. AND PART OF THE REASON WHY IS BECAUSE THIS REDUCTION OF PRICE REDUCTION IS GOING TO REDUCE A LOT OF PRESSURE OF THE CURRENT ACCOUNTS.

insights.claim: China needs fiscal rather than monetary policy to fix its two-tiered, weak-demand economy, but with exports still strong there is no urgency yet for major fiscal stimulus.
insights.speaker: Johanna (Head of Emerging Markets, Citigroup)
insights.so_what: Don't expect near-term big China fiscal stimulus while exports hold; the key catalyst to watch is fiscal policy, not LPR tweaks.
insights.quote: IT'S REALLY FISCAL POLICY WHICH IS A MAJOR FACTOR WE NEED TO WATCH FOR.

insights.claim: The dollar is strengthening on multiple drivers at once: rising oil (petrodollar), priced-in Fed hike bets, Iran tensions, UK political risk and yen rate differentials, making it the undisputed ultimate haven.
insights.speaker: Ruth Carson (Bloomberg, chief FX/rates correspondent Asia)
insights.so_what: A confluence of haven, rate-differential and petrodollar flows supports the dollar; the open question is whether the move has run too far.
insights.quote: YOU GO TO THE WORLD'S ULTIMATE HAVEN WHICH IS THE DOLLAR UNDISPUTEDLY.

insights.claim: With dollar-yen back above 161 despite a BOJ hike and hawkish deputy-governor comments, the market judges the BOJ has not done enough and Japanese intervention risk is real but of uncertain effectiveness.
insights.speaker: Ruth Carson (Bloomberg, chief FX/rates correspondent Asia)
insights.so_what: Yen weakness past 161 keeps intervention and a 2024-style carry-trade-unwind tail risk live; watch for verbal/actual MOF intervention.
insights.quote: THE BOJ HIKED, IT IS NOT ENOUGH. AT THE SAME TIME, YOU'VE GOT RATE DIFFERENTIALS REALLY WEIGHING ONCE AGAIN ON THE YEN.

insights.claim: UK PM Keir Starmer is expected to announce his future within hours and may set a path to stepping down after a heavy Andy Burnham by-election victory, potentially making Burnham the UK's seventh leader in a decade.
insights.speaker: Rosalind Mathieson (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: An imminent UK leadership change injects political risk into sterling and gilts; the key market question is fiscal discipline under a successor.
insights.quote: THE EXPECTATION IS NOW THAT KEIR STARMER WILL MAKE SOME KIND OF ANNOUNCEMENT WITHIN A MATTER OF HOURS MONDAY MORNING.

insights.claim: FX options imply a more than 60% chance the pound trades around 1.30 versus the dollar over coming months as investors question the UK's fiscal path under a potential Andy Burnham government.
insights.speaker: Ruth Carson (Bloomberg, chief FX/rates correspondent Asia)
insights.so_what: Options markets are already positioning for sterling downside to ~1.30; UK political/fiscal risk is the cleanest near-term short-the-pound expression.
insights.quote: THERE'S A MORE THAN 60% CHANCE THAT THE POUND COULD POTENTIALLY TRADE AROUND THE 130 MARK AGAINST THE DOLLAR OVER THE COMING MONTHS.

insights.claim: Andy Burnham has historically been on the spend-more left but has recently shifted to pledge fiscal discipline to reassure the gilt market, leaving doubt over whether he can hold the line against factional pressure to spend.
insights.speaker: Rosalind Mathieson (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Gilt investors should watch whether Burnham's fiscal-discipline pivot survives factional pressure; a relapse toward spending pressures gilts.
insights.quote: HE'S BEEN QUITE CAREFUL IN RECENT MONTHS TO SHIFT HIMSELF AWAY FROM THOSE NARRATIVES AND SAY HE WOULD CREATE FISCAL DISCIPLINE.

insights.claim: China added 46 US firms (including USA Rare Earths Inc.) to its export-control list banning government purchases, escalating the rare-earths trade conflict as the G7 targets cutting China dependence below 60%.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: Tit-for-tat export controls keep the rare-earths/critical-minerals supply-chain decoupling theme live as a geopolitical trade driver.
insights.quote: THEY HAVE ADDED NOW SOME U.S. FIRMS TO ITS EXPORT CONTROL LIST , ONE OF WHICH IS USA RARE EARTHS INC. TO THAT LIST. IT IS A TOTAL OF 46 U.S. FIRMS

insights.claim: Hong Kong is in talks with Chinese authorities to expand cross-border investment channels and let mainland retail investors subscribe to Hong Kong IPOs, with proposals to lower entry thresholds for qualified investors.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: Expanded mainland access to HK IPOs is a structural liquidity tailwind for Hong Kong brokerages and the IPO market.
insights.quote: HONG KONG IS IN TALKS WITH CHINESE AUTHORITIES TO EXPAND CROSS-BORDER INVESTMENT CHANNELS AND GRANT MAINLAND BUYERS ACCESS TO LOCAL IPOS.

insights.claim: Hong Kong's property recovery is judged more durable than the past 15 years because it is driven by population growth lifting rental yields above mortgage rates, not by liquidity or speculation.
insights.speaker: Cusson (CIO, KGI International Wealth Management)
insights.so_what: A fundamentals-driven (yield-over-mortgage) HK property recovery is a higher-quality, more sustainable long than prior speculative cycles.
insights.quote: IT WAS PURELY DRIVEN BY POPULATION IN HONG KONG INCREASING AND THERE IS DEMAND FOR HOUSING. THAT DRIVE WENT TO YIELD INCREASE TO SURPASS THE MORTGAGE RATE.

insights.claim: A threatened super El Nino, potentially the hottest year on record, feeds the higher-inflation/higher-rates narrative top-down and is highly stock-specific bottom-up, with losers in Peru's halted fishing industry and Indian sugar names exposed to only one cycle.
insights.speaker: guest, senior Asian equities reporter
insights.so_what: El Nino is both a macro inflation risk and a single-stock dispersion trade; favor diversified two-cycle agriculture players over single-cycle, low-inventory names.
insights.quote: THE FISHING INDUSTRY IN PERU HAS BEEN DISTURBED. IT HAS COME TO A HALT.

insights.claim: BYD is leading Chinese EV stocks lower, down ~4%, on an EU hybrid-cars tariff report, with similar declines across the sector.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: EU hybrid tariff risk is a fresh headwind for Chinese EV exporters; watch BYD and peers as the tariff details firm up.
insights.quote: B.Y.D. IS LEADING THE CHINESE E.V. STOCKS LOWER HERE. YOU'RE SEEING B.Y.D. DOWN 4%

insights.claim: Japan PM Takaichi said the consumption tax will return after a two-year cut period, a potential catalyst for Japanese consumption-related trades.
insights.speaker: Yvonne Man (host)
insights.so_what: A scheduled consumption-tax reinstatement is a forward headwind for Japanese consumer spending and related equities to position around.
insights.quote: THE PRIME MINISTER TAKAICHI, TALKING ABOUT A CONSUMPTION TAX WILL RETURN AFTER A TWO-YEAR CUT PERIOD.
